channel.title: llm on My learning and diary
channel.description: Recent content in llm on My learning and diary

item.title: From Conventional Commits to LLM-Generated Release Notes
item.description: Introduction For several years, I used Conventional Commits in my software projects. The idea was simple and practical: write commit messages in a structured format, then use tools to generate changelogs and release notes automatically. For example: feat: add user login fix: resolve payment retry issue docs: update API usage guide This worked well. Tools such as Conventional Changelog could read these commit messages and generate release notes based on the commit history.

item.title: Reducing Architecture Drift in Spec-Driven Development with coding agents and LLMs
item.description: Introduction Spec-driven development is becoming increasingly popular in the era of AI-assisted software engineering. Instead of starting directly from implementation, teams define specifications, domain rules, contracts, and architectural intentions first, allowing Large Language Models (LLMs) and automation tools to generate significant parts of the system. This approach can dramatically improve development speed, documentation quality, and alignment between business and engineering. However, one important challenge emerges quickly: Architecture drift.
